title: The Story of Winifred Eloise Coss

short.label: Short Summary
short.spoken: The Story of Winifred Eloise Coss is two great family adventures that finally meet. The Edith Powers branch begins in England around fifteen eighty-one with George Alcock. Puritan families crossed the Atlantic, survived frontier wars, the Salem witch panic, and the American Revolution. The Mary Ann Goodwater branch begins in Paris around fifteen seventy-five with Louis Hébert, France's first farmer in Canada. Those lines built Québec, endured brutal winters, and grew through the King's Daughters. In eighteen fifty-three, Joseph Warren Coss married Mary Ann Goodwater in Iowa, uniting the Powers and Goodwater branches. Generations later came Winifred Eloise Coss — raised in North Dakota through the Depression and Dust Bowl, married to Morris Rankin Batie in nineteen forty-two. This interactive storybook follows real relatives through famous moments in history — not names on a chart, but people who lived it.

medium.label: Medium Summary
medium.spoken:
The following paper is the story of Winifred Eloise Coss's family tree — two parallel journeys through four centuries that converge in Iowa and descend to North Dakota.

The Edith Powers branch begins in England. George Alcock was born in Cambridge in fifteen eighty-one. His descendants lived through the Protestant Reformation, England's wars with Scotland and France, and the decision to cross the Atlantic. Puritan families arrived with the Winthrop Fleet in sixteen thirty and helped build the Massachusetts Bay Colony. For generations they farmed the frontier: Barretts in Chelmsford, Sparks in King Philip's War, Powers on Neshoba Hill in Littleton. In sixteen ninety-one, Martha Barrett Sparks was accused of witchcraft and imprisoned in Boston while her husband fought away from home — a direct family tie to the Salem witch panic. Walter Powers married Rebecca Barrett; their son Josiah was born in a garrison house in seventeen ten. Later, Josiah Powers Jr. enlisted in New Hampshire regiments during the Revolutionary War. Warren Coss married Edith Powers in eighteen twenty-four, and their son Joseph Warren Coss was born in eighteen twenty-seven.

The Mary Ann Goodwater branch opens in France. Louis Hébert, born in Paris in fifteen seventy-five, became the first French farmer in Canada. Jacques Cartier and Champlain claimed the St. Lawrence for France. Carpenters and masons from Perche helped raise Québec out of the forest. Filles à marier and King's Daughters sailed to a colony that needed families. Twenty-four King's Daughters connect directly to this tree; fourteen ancestors were founding families of Île d'Orléans. The Bonneau Huguenots, later known as Goodwater, endured Iroquois raids, the French and Indian War, and life under British rule before moving west.

On two April eighteen fifty-three, Joseph Warren Coss married Mary Ann Goodwater in Winneshiek County, Iowa — joining England and Québec into one American family. The Coss line pushed through Wisconsin and Minnesota. Joseph served in the First Minnesota Heavy Artillery during the Civil War. Edgar Putnam Coss and Sarah Pearson raised sons on Dakota farms through bank panics, drought, and the Dust Bowl.

Winifred Eloise Coss was born to Charles Whitcomb Coss and Ethel Raymond. After her mother's death, she was raised by her aunt and uncle, Lorraine and Winfield Geiger, through the Great Depression in crowded North Dakota households. Roomers and relatives shared roofs to survive drought, low grain prices, and the Dust Bowl winds. In nineteen forty-two she married Morris Rankin Batie in Las Vegas. Photographs in the original document show Morris and Winifred together and Winifred at Lake Mead. Henry the Eighth and Cartier, witch panic and world wars, share these pages with carpenters, mothers, and farmers — all preserved in twenty-eight chapters, a family tree, and an interactive map you can explore at your own pace.

full.label: Full Summary
full.spoken:
Winifred Eloise Coss's family tree is not a dry chart of names. It is two vast stories — one English, one French-Canadian — that travel separately across four hundred years of world history and join in an Iowa wedding before reaching Winifred herself in twentieth-century North Dakota.

The Edith Powers branch begins in Cambridgeshire, England, with George Alcock, born in fifteen eighty-one. His people lived through the religious upheavals of the Tudor and Stuart eras: Henry VIII's break with Rome, Martin Luther's Reformation, Calvin's influence, and the wars that pushed dissenting Protestants toward the sea. In sixteen thirty, ancestors sailed with the Winthrop Fleet as part of the Great Migration. They helped settle Salem and the Massachusetts Bay Colony — building farms, churches, and towns on land already long inhabited by Native nations. The Pequot War, King Philip's War, and decades of frontier life shaped every generation.

For generations the Powers line moved west within New England. Thomas Barrett and his family settled Chelmsford. Martha Barrett Sparks was accused of witchcraft in sixteen ninety-one and sent to a Boston prison on the twenty-eighth of October while Henry Sparks was away fighting King Philip's War. Her father petitioned the governor for her release in sixteen ninety-two — the family document records the accusation and imprisonment, a direct involvement in the Salem-era panic, not merely living nearby. Walter Powers married Rebecca Barrett; their son Josiah Powers Sr. was born in a garrison house on Neshoba Hill in Littleton in seventeen ten. Town officers, tithingmen, and soldiers followed: Zebadiah Johnson in Temple, New Hampshire; Josiah Powers Jr. enlisting in seventeen seventy-five and again in seventeen seventy-eight in Continental regiments against the British.

Warren Coss married Edith Powers in Lowell, Massachusetts, in eighteen twenty-four. Their son Joseph Warren Coss, born in eighteen twenty-seven, left New Hampshire for Vermont and then the western states — a thousand-mile migration repeated by generations. Edith Powers was born in Athens, Vermont, in eighteen hundred, beginning the formal join of the two great branches that would complete in Iowa.

The Mary Ann Goodwater branch begins across the Atlantic. Louis Hébert, born in Paris on nine July fifteen seventy-five, became the first French farmer in Canada. Jacques Cartier claimed the St. Lawrence for France; Samuel de Champlain's Québec grew with master carpenters like Zacharie Cloutier and masons like Jean Guyon, who dragged logs and stone before horses arrived. Robert Giffard's colonists from Perche signed contracts: work three years, receive land and animals. Filles à marier — marriageable young women — and later the King's Daughters answered France's call to populate the St. Lawrence valley. About seven hundred sixty-eight women were recruited; twenty-four King's Daughters connect directly to Winifred's tree. Fourteen ancestors were among the three hundred founding families of Île d'Orléans. The Bonneau Huguenots, persecuted in France, became the Goodwater line and cleared farms through Iroquois raids, the Beaver Wars, the French and Indian War, British conquest after seventeen sixty-three, and the long persistence of French culture under English rule.

The two branches met on two April eighteen fifty-three, when twenty-seven-year-old Joseph Warren Coss married fifteen-year-old Mary Ann Goodwater in Winneshiek County, Iowa. That wedding joined the massive Powers and Goodwater trees into one Coss family — Puritan New England and French Canada in a single American story. They pushed west through Wisconsin and Minnesota. Joseph left the household before eighteen fifty, moving farther west to Winneshiek County. He enlisted as a private in Company M of the First Minnesota Heavy Artillery during the Civil War at age thirty-eight. Edgar Putnam Coss, born in eighteen fifty-five, married Sarah Pearson and raised sons Charles, Clifford, and Clarence through prosperity, the eighteen ninety-three panic, and the hard years that followed. Scottish Falconers and French Carrières married into the line, adding still more threads from Inverness and the St. Lawrence.

Winifred Eloise Coss appears near the end of this arc. She was born to Charles Whitcomb Coss and Ethel Cannon Raymond in North Dakota. When she was five, after her mother Ethel died in nineteen twenty-seven, her father placed her with his sister Lorraine Powers Geiger and brother-in-law Winfield Geiger — nicknamed Happy. Census records show her growing up in crowded households through the Great Depression and Dust Bowl: roomers helping cover rent, tight budgets, and farm communities struggling while Clarence and Charles Coss worked as firemen in Grand Forks. In the nineteen thirty census she was seven in Cavalier; in nineteen forty she was seventeen in Kenmare. On twenty-five April nineteen forty-two she married Morris Rankin Batie in Las Vegas, where he worked as an air traffic controller. A photograph preserves her at Lake Mead in those years — a young woman at the edge of the desert, at the edge of a world war. The document that preserves all of this was written for her: a paper that turns a table of contents, census lines, and ship lists into a story a grandchild can hear aloud.

That is the shape of this project. Henry VIII and Cartier, Washington's armies and Lincoln's regiments, Champlain and the filles du roi share the narrative with garrison births, river contracts, petitions for an imprisoned aunt, and teenagers choosing husbands in Canada. Famous people open the chapters; your relatives walk through them. You can read each section, click a purple family name to open the tree, slide the timeline under the map, or ask a question in your own words and hear the answer read back. None of that replaces the paper your family already wrote — it makes the paper walk and talk. Twenty-eight tightened sections, more than a hundred and thirty photographs, an interactive family tree, and a map across centuries from fourteen sixty-nine to nineteen fifty show how Winifred Eloise Coss's people fit into history — and how history fit into them.
